dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 6 at --Address Redacted-- for chest pain.
Hi, my name is Angela, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I was having chest pain, nausea, dizziness, and also vomiting all morning. It's been rough.
Can you tell me your name and the date today?
I'm Victoria. Today is February 4th.
What city or town are you in right now?
I'm in [CITY], and the pain and vomiting forced me to call.
Your airway is clear. Is there anything in your throat or mouth?
Assessing your breathing now.
Your breathing is 16 per minute and even.
Are you having trouble catching your breath or chest discomfort?
I'm going to check your pulse and blood pressure now.
Your pulse is 76, strong and regular.
Your blood pressure is 79 over 48. We'll monitor that.
Your skin is pale, warm, and dry.
Can you describe your chest pain from 0 to 10?
It's eight. It's sharp and pressure-like, right in the middle.
When did your symptoms start? And have they changed?
They started this morning with nausea, vomiting, and dizziness, and they've been getting worse.
How many times did you vomit today?
Five times this morning. I've also had polyuria, malaise, and nausea for the last three days, but today is the worst. I haven't taken my medications today, though I was able to take them last night.
What medications are you usually on?
I'm on Lantus, NovoLog, Plavix, carvedilol, sertraline, gabapentin, iron, aspirin, levoxyl, Lorazepam, Zanaflex, and a multivitamin.
Any chronic illnesses or past hospital visits?
I have an old heart attack, high blood pressure, diabetes, a stroke, endometriosis, had my gallbladder removed, chronic pancreatitis, and hyperkalemia.
Have you had any history of appendicitis?
Yes, I had surgery for appendicitis in the past.
Do you have any medication allergies?
I'm allergic to fentanyl, tramadol, nitroglycerin, Toradol, and dimenhydrinate.
We'll check your blood sugar now.
Your blood sugar is 480. That's high and may be contributing to your symptoms.
Your oxygen is 99% right now.
We're going to follow the chest pain protocol.
We're placing you on the cardiac monitor now.
We're starting a portable cardiac monitor. Is that okay?
We've applied the 4-lead monitor.
The 4-lead shows sinus rhythm with no ectopy.
Now we are performing a 12-lead EKG.
The 12-lead shows a normal sinus rhythm with no immediate cardiac concerns.
We need to start an IV. Can we use your arm?
We've placed a 20-gauge IV in your right arm successfully.
We'll give 400 mL of normal saline over transport to help with hydration and lower your blood sugar.
Your pulse is now 69, strong and regular.
Your blood pressure is 90 over 58 and continuing to improve.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Your GCS is 15. That's good - you are fully alert.
You mentioned a prior stroke. Have you had any recent neurological changes like difficulty speaking or recognizing objects?
No, nothing like that right now.
Your pulse is now 67, and still regular.
Your blood pressure has improved to 127 over 69.
Your oxygen saturation is now 100%, showing improvement.
Let me check your oxygen again; it's staying stable at 100%.
We'll be taking you to the hospital. Which one, [COMPANY_NAME] or elsewhere?
[COMPANY_NAME] is fine.
We are now preparing to place you in semi-Fowler's position for transport.
We'll keep you in semi-Fowler's position with shoulder and lap belts for safety during transport.
The pre-arrival report has been placed with the hospital. They're expecting us.
We're ready to hand you over to the hospital staff. They've been alerted. You're in good care.
The normal saline was administered over transport, and your blood pressure improved significantly.
We administered normal saline, and your IV line remains in place for the hospital to continue.
We also provided your medications and allergy list to ensure there are no mistakes with your treatment.
Additionally, your past medical history includes a stroke. We checked for any neurological issues and found nothing concerning.
The hospital knows you'll need insulin and hydration on arrival.
We've applied shoulder and lap belts to your stretcher to keep you secure.
Your IV will continue to run once we arrive.
Our patient remains stable on monitored IV normal saline and we expect a quick and safe transport.
I've secured the stretcher during transport for your safety.
We have the IV fluids running, and your condition seems stable for transport.
Hospital staff have been informed that we suspect hyperglycemia and dehydration.
We did administer IV normal saline and have already recorded an O2 saturation of 94 during transport.
I'll let them know we've started a slow NS drip for hydration and to begin glucose control.
Your IV allows us to deliver fluids quickly, so they can regulate the rate when we arrive.
We'll follow up if you need anything else before we arrive.
That's it. We're ready for the ride over.
Let's go.
I've already secured my attention to the ambulance during transport. I'll manage the IV and monitor flow rate.
Since you mentioned being anxious, we'll let the hospital team know you may need extra reassurance.
Also, let's keep your right arm uncovered for IV access. It's important for continued management in the ER.
We'll monitor your vital signs en route and provide updates if anything changes.
We'll meet you outside the hospital and help transfer you safely.
Your IV is running at 400 mL over transport. Will they want to continue this?
We'll make sure they know to continue the fluids and monitor your hydration and glucose.
